speaker: interviewer
text: --an interview with Henry Wermuth. And this is tape number 12.

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably the last. [LAUGHS] Anyway, I joined a-- about a thousand youngsters all naked and practically all Muselmann on the way out. Our clothes, our so-called pajama suits were taken away. And whereas in previous camps I can't recall having had lice, but in this particular one, on the train, I had-- one of my main jobs was killing lice. That is by the by. So I took away. And I never give it back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went into the barracks. And in the middle was a door. And it went in two parts-- probably 500 in each. I calculated that because-- well, I'll tell you that in a minute. But before that, I must have done something wrong because one man dressed-- took me back into the previous room and asked me to kneel on a-- I'm not sure what I have done wrong-- on a-- on a stool.

speaker: interviewee
text: And before he sort of-- I want to make it a little bit shorter now and quicker. Before long, I managed to run away. And he wouldn't sort of find me amongst the others, anyway. And I went through a door. Now, having come late there, I had to join four others in a bed what is-- which is normally meant for one-- four. And they were all crowded with fours and fives. And thought I was clever when I managed to be on the side where we, like sardines-- three on one side, two on the other-- was on the side where there was only one more. But when their legs all came on top of us, I don't-- I didn't think it was so clever anymore. Because the more on the other side, the more legs come on top of you. But our legs-- anyway, and the worst of that was that one of them had diarrhea. The next day, I asked them, if they give me the blanket-- the only they had-- I will get out of the bed. And next to the bed was a table. And kneeling on the table was-- because it wasn't big enough-- and with my hands on my-- my head on my folded arms, that's how I slept the next night. And I'm not sure if another night after that. But shortly, the man with the diarrhea-- a man came and put the name, and I'm not sure how he knew his name-- L-E-V-I on his chest. Now, why he did that, I don't know. But this man was taken away soon, the one with the diarrhea. And I was glad to go back into the bed with four-- I mean, now we were only four. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, now the Russians were, of course-- first they were like Auschwitz. And then they got smaller. Then there was a loaf of bread for four. And then it was for six, then for eight, and even for 10, and maybe nothing. But ice developed, you know, again. [LAUGHS] Let me explain it a different way. I liked conjuring tricks as a child. And it came, sort of, to my help at this particular moment and sometimes also during the war, war years. A, a big can of soup for, for the barracks were put in the middle of the barracks. The beds were, like, three high-- three beds high, containing between 12 and 15 people. And next to it, directly next to each other, another one containing-- and then there was a little gap, and so on. That's why I calculated the amount of people. And they were dying by the hour, more and more. And in-between, there was a walkway between one side and the other. And that's where the soup was put. I had, where the table was next to mine, a little bit of a bigger gap because there was a window between me and the next bed on this side.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went, I took the blanket, and I walked past the container with hot watery soup-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and with the idea of-- if they can live on a little bit of watery soup, if I get a couple of spoons more, I will survive better. I would say now that it is-- was more the spirit than anything else that helped me survive. Now, I made a few passes near this container. At the third or fourth time, with a blanket on my right side sort of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] over it, I bent down and open the container. Next, I went to my bed, took my soup-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] soup disk? What is it? What is soup-- Dish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dish-- soup dish, metal soup dish. And while I was passing again, lifted it, and scooped, you know, some soup out of it, very hot, scalding my thumb. But who cares? Now, I thought because of the blanket on top that nobody had seen it. And slowly, I made my way to the nearby bed, took out-- took my spoon. But I couldn't eat before everybody else. So I bent down on my knees and wondered-- with the head under the bed, intended to eat my soup. But in no time, I was surrounded by a herd of growling wolves. I had no alternative but to sit on the floor with the dish in front of me. And I think I got but two or three spoons full. And then it had disappeared. But they let me lick it out. I was sort of-- wiped with the finger.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next day, I could swear there were hundreds of pairs of eyes rather-- when the-- when the-- when the soup came-- trained on me than-- rather than watching the soup container. What would I do? I didn't move. And our line was first to be given soup. I didn't move.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had my soup. They went to the end of the barracks. But before they turned back, I nipped over into another bed where I discovered there were only two. But their eyes were big. And that's the only thing that seems to be alive there. One was-- this was the middle of the beds of the three-tier beds. And he-- one had to stoop. And they growled when I entered the bed. And I said, I'm going to give you a part, so there, keep quiet. There was no talk. It was growling. There was-- I don't know, no human noises anyway. And it was-- well, when it came, they couldn't-- they had-- they had-- you know, spoons of whatever it was, I'm not sure. But I had to feed them because there were sitting the others at the end of the bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one for you, one for you, one for me. And when it wasn't quite full-- the spoon-- I heard another growl. I had to do better next time. And probably the most macabre dinner anybody could think of.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were looking like dead people. And the last chapter was-- I called, 'Finale-- the Living Dead.' And these were the best example. And during the last few days, they died. Anyway, those were the last few days. Now, the only thing I will-- because I mentioned before-- were the toilets. Most people had diarrhea and couldn't hold it all the-- all the way and lost a lot before the toilets. I was practically more to the middle. The stench still, still reached me. But it was much stronger near the toilets. There were actually-- actual sort of cubicles with doors. But we were not bothering about that anymore. The doors were never shut. And the doors were never blocked because there was nothing to block them, no paper or anything. And-- but to find spaces to walk without making your feet dirty was practically impossible. And on the way back were the first-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on the floor, space on the floor, I found which bit of dirt has finished. I wipe my feet, like, on a mat. And that was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, it was the 5th of May now. And on the way back from the toilet, a prisoner stood on the table. And he shouted-- was I on the toilet, or was in the bed? I can't even remember at the time when he shouted that. In slightly imperfect German, [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, most probably, if he had said it in normal circumstances, everybody would have jumped and shouted and screamed-- not a sound in the barracks. There were people, three in a bed. And next to them was an empty bed. They were not even bothering anymore to move into the empty one. That's how it was. I pulled the blanket over my head. And in contrast to the one tear which I had at the time when my father died, I had a stream of tears and convulsions. I, I didn't know where they came from. The whole thing broke-- the whole-- everything that you could think or not think of the brain can't take as much came on to me. And the only thing I said to myself, alone, probably telling myself that I am alone. And perhaps that was one of the times when I really had given up because it only-- it was so bad already that, you know-- my surroundings-- that even if anybody would be alive, it would be a miracle. And the others-- the others died away like flies anyway. But I had a feeling I had to share-- oh, if I only had my father to share with him. I wanted to share with someone. And I looked at my bedmate, the only one left. The others had died. But he was dead as well. The next thing-- the Americans couldn't probably do what they wanted to do to feed us as quickly as they want to do. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but the war was over-- the, the, the, the, the camp-- there, I'm sure the SS had fled, as I say, a long time ago. I don't know who was in charge of it. I don't even know-- remember if we had eaten something. But some who could walk better-- I couldn't walk anymore. I just sort of step off the steps lightly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't go out of the barracks. But I saw at the back of the barracks-- outside of where the barracks were, past the toilets, I saw a little fire-- around the fire a prisoner with a pot and about 3 or 4 potatoes in it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had to what mention one more thing, which-- because it's the title of my next book. I have to mention it. At the-- at first, there were the sayings, 'If it only doesn't get worse,' you've heard-- you remember that. The next thing was-- you heard-- it was the last thing, the phrase, 'Oh, to survive, be it only with a dry, plain potato.' It was-- that was in Yiddish, so that translation would be 'dry.' But the meaning would be 'a plain potato,' in other words, the minimum of sustenance to survive. People pledged not to want any more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that is why I made it-- the plain potato-- I made it the title of my second book, that I adhered to that pledge. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] thing was that I saw this man having cooked his potatoes. I was crawling on all fours, leaving my blanket behind because I didn't want to have it get dirty on the dry spot. It would-- nobody would come to steal it anymore. Nobody moved anymore, practically, very few. And wading through the stuff, practically, and getting near to him like a wildcat-- and as he was taking the first potato to his mouth, I was grabbing it. And not being able to run, I shoved away on all fours. He was angry, but didn't want to go after me because he would have, have to leave his potatoes behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took the potatoes out. And before I was very far, threw the boiling water. If I would have had a full hit, it would have been my end. It was a near death-- a near-- a near miss, just scalding my soles a little bit with a few drops. And, as I say, the first meal I had was a dry-- a plain potato. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, well, what can I say?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Americans took me afterwards to a hospital tent, where we were-- we had no beds. They were sleeping on stretchers. And I was wounded everywhere because the bones were sticking out. I had no meat. But I didn't know that really. I didn't look at myself, you know. It was-- my skin was parchment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a young boy next to me-- they gave him some of probably internal feeding or blood, I'm not sure what. He was dead the next morning, before I asked me, what will Americans do with me? I got tablets-- and probably to stop my diarrhea. But the first-- then the chemists, they came again with soup. What was it? A watery soup again with a few more noodles, with something swimming in it, but a bit more. So I could have a few portions.

speaker: interviewee
text: I poured away the water and had a few noodles, and gradually sort of-- if my father asked me-- I felt as if my father would give me the advice again, don't eat. So I ate very slowly, very little. And probably that saved me. Because the doctor-- the [NON-ENGLISH] doctor came. He only asked me what I had. And I said, I had diarrhea. And he gave me something. Otherwise-- because there were too many, he would say, probably to himself, either he makes it or he doesn't. Then I get out of the hospital. And I passed by through the barracks. And I'm not sure who it was, but I'm telling you now it was before or after that because I'm hurrying up because it should be the last tape. And I walked with a stick outside the front of the barracks. And I saw two very large heaps-- pyramids of corpses in all sort of-- entangled. And I was for a second thinking, maybe my father is amongst them. But I couldn't stop. I couldn't stop. I had to go on-- go on, sort of shuffle on slowly, bit by bit. And then I came across [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a barracks with darkened windows. And I looked inside, hoping maybe to find something to eat. It was darkened. And I noticed another skeletal figure. And as I drew nearer, the figure came closer with identical movements. I didn't recognize it at first. But obviously it was-- that's the first I saw myself after a long time, another skeletal figure. But instead of my spirits sagging at that time, it lifted. And I waved to this image. And it smiled back when I said, this Muselmann is going to live. For the rest, I was six weeks in this particular camp. And from there, we had the opportunity to go with the Jewish Brigade [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who registers that I could go to Israel or to Palestine. I didn't want to go because I imagined I would have to stay-- stand next to my religious uncle, stand in prayers with somebody who hasn't experienced what I had experienced and that didn't want to know. But-- and I did register. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Jewish Brigade took us. A column of 35 trucks, lorries, went.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then at one time-- it was six weeks later, and I'd had a bit of growth of hair. And suddenly, we were asked to let down the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what it called? The-- what is it made of? Tent plants-- what is it made of? But anyway, the material we just-- Oh, canvas.

speaker: interviewee
text: Canvas, you know. It's bad for a writer to think of words quickly enough. But it happens. Anyway-- laid down the canvas.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was actually angry because we go through the border into Italy. I thought, what's happening? Although the spirit of adventure to being smuggled, you know, gripped me. I liked adventure, but, you know, not the type which I had before, but where the cards are evenly stacked. And I was very angry. Who do they think there are, the Italians, to stop us going home to our land? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't think-- I never thought of it until much, much later, perhaps even when I wrote, that it was perhaps, perhaps the British who shouldn't find out that we were going to Palestine. Because the Arabs complained, and it was probably more for the British not to know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we ended up in Italy, my first-- you know, in a country where you could only dream about before, you know, Italy, Italy-- nowadays you can go whenever-- and palm trees and people walking there. And there were 35 lorries. And I was the only one who went on top into the-- on the soft roof of the lorry and saw the scenery from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now there, more or less, my book ends because a new life of adventure starts. And how much have you still got?

speaker: interviewer
text: We have eight minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight minutes. Well, during the six weeks still in the camp, I got myself an outfit-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] short trousers; a shirt, which I didn't know whether it was gray or brown or both, no buttons; and a green straw hat; no socks, but ankle-high boots. [LAUGHS] Surely a very funny outfit.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it's how I walked-- in Italy, walked into a rather posh cafe, people sitting outside. And they're looking at me. And how could I explain to them that I am richer than they are? I am alive. And their amused glances did not hurt me. They amused me as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we were taken to a camp in Modena. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was also the trickery I mentioned to get more than my ration, you know, and borrowing other people's ration cards and showing them in a way that they couldn't see that they were stamped already. It's, you know, it's conjuring. Maybe you may call it not nice. But it was-- my spirits to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: --get-- survive, carried on. And I must say this, that when in six months in Italy-- oh, I didn't go to Palestine. Many, many, many years later I went. Of course, it was by then Israel, and for a long time. So-- and, well, I gained-- in six months, I gained about a hundred weight. When I was liberated, I weighed half-- about half my weight or less, around 30 or 32 kilograms, which was less than half my weight. And every doctor will tell you that nobody can go back to less your half weight and live.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so this is, more or less, where I think I will end my story because what comes now goes into my second book. And was it worse to survive? When I am asked this question, I would say there was one event worth to survive, maybe even two.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the first one didn't last long, only 50 years. The partition of Germany was won and the regaining-- [CRYING] I'm even moved now by the idea, at the time, of our homeland of Israel in 1948-- was the second one and the main one, which was worthwhile to surviving for. When they said, 'Never again,' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know what they mean. My country, right or wrong-- my country. And if people sometimes say-- when you hear sometimes, deeds which were not, perhaps, to the modern standard of what one should or should not do, don't forget they only do it there in revenge. It was done to them first, not by the Germans, in this case-- by the Arabs. Shalom.
